content.problemStatement:
Alice Potter's fifth year at Hogwarts is coming to an end, which means it's time for the important OWL exam. Help Alice pass the OWL in numerology, the science of numbers, and operations on them.

The exam paper contains a sequence AAA of NNN integers.

There are two types of tasks:


Given iii and XXX, set AiA_iAi​ equal to XXX.
Given integers LLL and RRR (1≤L≤R≤N)(1 \leq L \leq R \leq N)(1≤L≤R≤N), find XXX and YYY (L≤X≤Y≤R)(L \leq X \leq Y \leq R)(L≤X≤Y≤R) such that the value of ∣sin⁡(AX+AX+1+⋯+AY)°∣| \sin (A_X+A_{X+1}+\dots+A_Y) \degree |∣sin(AX​+AX+1​+⋯+AY​)°∣ is **minimum** possible.
Note that ∣sin⁡(x)°∣|\sin(x)\degree|∣sin(x)°∣ denotes the absolute value of sine of x°x\degreex°.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ — the number of elements in the sequence and the number of tasks in the exam.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the sequence.
Each of the next QQQ lines contains three integers — descriptions of the tasks: 111 iii XXX, or 222 LLL RRR, where the first number indicates the type of task, and the next two indicate contents of the task.


Output Format
For each task of the second type, output two space-separated integers XXX and YYY (L≤X≤Y≤R)(L \leq X \leq Y \leq R)(L≤X≤Y≤R) — the answer to the task.

If there are several answers, print any one of them.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T — the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains two integers N and Q — the length of the array and the number of tasks. The second line contains N integers A1 … AN — the initial array. Each of the next Q lines describes a task. A task of type 1 is given as "1 i X" (set Ai = X). A task of type 2 is given as "2 L R" (find X,Y with L ≤ X ≤ Y ≤ R that minimises |sin((AX+…+AY)°)|).
content.outputFormat: For each task of type 2, output a line with two integers X and Y (L ≤ X ≤ Y ≤ R) that achieve the minimum possible value. If several pairs give the same minimum, output any of them.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first query asks for the sub‑array inside [1,4] with minimum |sin|. The sub‑array consisting of the single element 361 gives sum 361°, whose sine is close to 0, so (3,3) is printed. The second query examines [1,2]; the best sub‑array is the whole range (1,2) with sum -3°, giving the smallest absolute sine. After updating A2 to 11, the third query on [1,2] chooses the single element at position 2 (value 11) because its sine is smaller than that of the element 12. After setting A3 to 47, the final query on [1,4] picks the single element at position 4 (value 5) as it yields the smallest |sin| among all possible sub‑arrays.

solution.approach: Because N and Q are at most 15 (as required by the meta‑rules), we can answer each query by brute force. For a type‑2 query we enumerate all sub‑arrays inside [L,R], compute their sum, evaluate |sin(sum°)| using the standard library (convert degrees to radians), and keep the pair with the smallest value. For a type‑1 query we simply update the array element. The overall complexity per test case is O(Q·N^2), which is trivial for N ≤ 15.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – the array and a few auxiliary variables.
